title: Celiac disease: villous atrophy of the small intestine
vignette: A 30-year-old man comes to the office due to a 4-month history of fatigue that is worse at the end of the day. The patient has also had abdominal bloating and intermittent loose stools after meals. His appetite is normal, and he has had no weight loss. Vital signs are normal. Examination shows pale mucosa. The abdomen is mildly distended and tympanic; there is no tenderness or organomegaly. Rectal examination shows brown stool that is negative for fecal occult blood. Laboratory results are as follows: hematocrit 28%, mean corpuscular volume 75 um3, leukocytes 7,100/mm3, platelets 490,000/mm3. C-reactive protein level is normal. Which of the following pathologic gastrointestinal tract findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

clues.text: abdominal bloating and intermittent loose stools after meals
clues.note: Postprandial bloating and loose stools with a normal appetite suggest malabsorption. In a young adult with iron-deficiency anemia and no inflammation, celiac disease is the leading cause.
Pallor reflects anemia. Combined with the microcytic indices below, this is iron-deficiency anemia - a common presentation of celiac disease (impaired duodenal iron absorption).

Microcytic anemia (MCV 75 / normal 80-100) with low hematocrit and reactive thrombocytosis, in the absence of GI blood loss (negative occult blood), points to iron malabsorption - classic for celiac disease.

A normal CRP argues against inflammatory bowel disease/colitis. This steers the diagnosis toward a non-inflammatory malabsorptive process - celiac disease with villous atrophy - rather than Crohn or microscopic colitis.

whatMatters: A young adult with malabsorptive symptoms, iron-deficiency (microcytic) anemia, no GI bleeding, and a normal CRP has celiac disease. The pathologic finding is atrophy of the small-intestinal mucosa with loss of villi - answer A.
strategy: For malabsorption + microcytic (iron-deficiency) anemia without bleeding in a young adult, think celiac disease: duodenal villous blunting impairs iron absorption. Match the histology - villous atrophy with crypt hyperplasia and intraepithelial lymphocytes (small intestine) - and rule out colonic processes (normal CRP, no blood).

options.text: Atrophic mucosa with loss of villi in the small intestine
Autoimmune destruction of gastric parietal cells
Chronic inflammation within the colonic mucosa and submucosa
Giardia trophozoites lining the small intestinal mucosa
Subepithelial collagen deposition in the colon

eliminations.label: parietal cell destruction - would cause B12 (macrocytic) anemia
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: autoimmune gastritis destroying parietal cells -> loss of intrinsic factor -> B12 deficiency (pernicious anemia). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that causes MACROCYTIC anemia, but this patient is MICROCYTIC (iron deficiency); the location/anemia type don't fit. ✅ Correct if: macrocytic anemia with low B12 and anti-parietal/IF antibodies.

eliminations.label: colonic transmural/mucosal inflammation - IBD, CRP would be up
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: chronic colonic inflammation (inflammatory bowel disease). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: CRP is normal and there's no blood in stool; the picture is non-inflammatory small-bowel malabsorption, not colitis. ✅ Correct if: elevated CRP, bloody diarrhea, and colonic involvement.

eliminations.label: Giardia - usually acute/travel-related, not this chronic iron-deficiency picture
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: giardiasis causes small-bowel malabsorption with trophozoites on biopsy. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it typically follows contaminated-water/travel exposure with acute-subacute foul diarrhea, not chronic iron-deficiency anemia from villous atrophy; nothing suggests exposure here. ✅ Correct if: camping/travel exposure with steatorrhea and trophozoites/cysts identified.

eliminations.label: subepithelial collagen in colon - collagenous colitis, wrong demographic/labs
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: collagenous (microscopic) colitis - watery diarrhea with a thickened subepithelial collagen band, typically in older women. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it doesn't cause iron-deficiency anemia or affect the small bowel; this young man's microcytic anemia and malabsorption fit celiac. You chose this. ✅ Correct if: chronic watery diarrhea in an older woman with normal endoscopy but collagen band on biopsy.

reasoning: Young adult, malabsorptive symptoms, iron-deficiency (microcytic) anemia
No GI blood loss (negative occult blood); normal CRP (non-inflammatory)
Celiac disease: gluten-driven small-bowel mucosal damage
Histology = villous atrophy (with crypt hyperplasia, intraepithelial lymphocytes) -> answer A
mechanism: Celiac disease is an immune reaction to gliadin (gluten) in genetically predisposed (HLA-DQ2/DQ8) individuals. Tissue transglutaminase-modified gliadin peptides activate CD4 T cells, driving mucosal injury concentrated in the duodenum/proximal small intestine: villous atrophy (blunting), crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes. Loss of absorptive surface impairs iron (and later folate/fat-soluble vitamin) uptake, producing iron-deficiency microcytic anemia and malabsorptive symptoms without colonic inflammation. Diagnosis is by anti-tTG/anti-endomysial IgA antibodies and confirmatory small-bowel biopsy; treatment is a gluten-free diet.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid GI/Pathology > Celiac disease: HLA-DQ2/DQ8, anti-tTG/anti-endomysial/anti-DGP antibodies, duodenal villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + intraepithelial lymphocytes, iron/folate malabsorption, dermatitis herpetiformis, gluten-free diet.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Synthesize the labs: microcytic anemia + malabsorption + no bleeding + normal CRP -> small-bowel villous atrophy. Then choose the histology that matches.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on villous atrophy, malabsorption, microcytic vs macrocytic anemia, intraepithelial lymphocytes, collagenous colitis? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: You chose E (collagenous colitis). That's a COLONIC, watery-diarrhea diagnosis in older women without iron deficiency. The iron-deficiency + small-bowel malabsorption picture is celiac -> villous atrophy.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Anchor: young adult + iron-deficiency anemia + bloating/loose stools + normal CRP = celiac (duodenal villous atrophy).

keywords.front: Malabsorption + iron-deficiency anemia + villous atrophy, young adult

keywords.back: Villous atrophy + crypt hyperplasia + intraepithelial lymphocytes (duodenum)
Watery diarrhea, older women, subepithelial collagen band, normal endoscopy

firstAid.detail: GI: pathogenesis, duodenal villous atrophy, serology, gluten-free diet, complications.
firstAid.location: GI: pathogenesis, duodenal villous atrophy, serology, gluten-free diet, complications.

firstAid.detail: GI/Heme: iron-deficiency from malabsorption vs blood loss; reactive thrombocytosis.
firstAid.location: GI/Heme: iron-deficiency from malabsorption vs blood loss; reactive thrombocytosis.

firstAid.detail: GI: autoimmune gastritis, IBD, giardiasis, microscopic (collagenous/lymphocytic) colitis.
firstAid.location: GI: autoimmune gastritis, IBD, giardiasis, microscopic (collagenous/lymphocytic) colitis.

memoryHook: Celiac = gluten flattens the villi. Lost absorptive carpet -> iron can't get in -> microcytic anemia.
